interactions.summary: A student reads Virgil's account of limbo: virtuous spirits lack baptism, have no hope, and live in longing despite having committed no evil.
Jiang rejects Virgil's self-excusing account of limbo, saying Virgil chooses to stay in hell despite many paths toward salvation.
A student reads the Minos passage, where Minos warns Dante to be careful whom he trusts and Virgil interrupts by invoking the will above.
Jiang interprets Minos's warning as a warning against Virgil: in hell nothing is what it seems, and the person closest to Dante is the one readers should distrust.
Jiang prompts Ivory to read; this is classroom coordination, not a substantive question.

A student reads Virgil's naming of famous souls in the circle of lust, including Semiramis, Cleopatra, Helen, Achilles, Paris, and Tristan, while an unnamed spirit who killed herself for love remains unspoken.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 is quoted Dante material, not original student commentary.

claims.claim: Virgil's claim that he is merely unlucky in limbo is incorrect; Jiang says Virgil chooses to be there and has paths toward salvation that he refuses.
Minos's warning to be careful whom Dante trusts only makes sense, in Jiang's reading, as a warning not to trust Virgil.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of Minos passage stated on 2026-04-08.

claims.claim: Virgil's interruption of Minos confirms the warning because he silences the figure who is trying to alert Dante.
In hell, nothing is what it seems, and Dante plants clues that the person readers most trust, Virgil, is probably the one they should least trust.
Virgil names many souls in lust but leaves unnamed the spirit who killed herself for love, setting up the Dido paradox continued in the next packet.
claims.temporal_scope: Observation from the quoted passage, interpreted on 2026-04-08.

signature_moments.moment: Minos's warning becomes a hidden warning about Virgil: do not trust the person closest to you.
signature_moments.source_phrase: don't trust the person closest to you
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes the trusted guide the dangerous intimate authority.

signature_moments.moment: Hell is the place where nothing is what it seems, so every claim by Virgil has to be questioned.
signature_moments.source_phrase: in hell, nothing is what it seems
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the episode its hermeneutic rule: suspicion is part of the journey.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Minos's warning to be careful whom Dante trusts, read by Jiang as a warning against Virgil.
Jiang's rule that nothing in Inferno is what it seems, requiring suspicion toward Virgil.

chronology_notes.note: The 2026-04-08 lecture intensifies the previous packet's unreliable-guide thesis by using both limbo and Minos as evidence against Virgil.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript renders Minos as 'Mino' and has some ASR damage in the quoted reading; semantic extraction keeps the passage-level meaning.
